10. Place a kitchen towel on a rimless baking sheet that’s at least 18 inches long (or, if you don’t have one, an upside-down rimmed baking sheet). Generously dust the towel with flour and set aside.
11. Lightly flour the work surface, then use the scraper to quickly lift one of the cylinders and turn it over onto the floured work surface so it's seam side up on the floured surface. Very lightly dust the dough with flour and use floured hands to pat it down gently, popping any larger bubbles and flattening it into a rectangle with the short ends oriented to the left and right. Fold the long end opposite you down over the bottom half, leaving the lower third of the dough uncovered, then fold up the uncovered third of dough so you have a long cylinder of dough with a seam running through the center. Starting at one end and working your way to the other, fold the dough in half lengthwise by bringing the top half down over the bottom and pressing firmly along the seam to seal. Throughout the shaping process, if the dough sticks to your hands or the surface, dust with just a bit more flour. Applying light downward pressure with both palms and starting in the center and working outward, roll out the dough to a length of 18 inches, making it as even as possible and tapering the ends.
12. Transfer the shaped dough to the floured towel, placing it seam side up in the middle. Pleat the towel on either side of the dough to create a barrier, then repeat the shaping process with the two remaining portions of dough and place them on the floured towel on either side of the first so all three pieces are sitting snugly next to one another. Fold the ends of the towel up and over the dough to cover. (Use a second towel if necessary.)
13. Let the baguettes sit at warm room temperature until they’re about 50 percent expanded, 30-60 min.  (It should take a couple of seconds to spring back and holds a slight indentation after being poked).
14. While the dough is proofing, bring 1 cup water to a boil in a kettle or saucepan, then set aside. Place a piece of parchment paper about the size of your baking stone over a rimless baking sheet or a thin wooden board and dust lightly with semolina flour.
15. Uncover the dough and pull on the ends of the towel to flatten the pleats, separating the loaves. Lightly dust the tops of the dough with flour. Working one at a time and using the towel to help you, flip one of the outer baguettes onto a baguette peel, then slide it gently onto the parchment paper, orienting it lengthwise. Repeat with the remaining loaves.
16. Use a razor blade to score each baguette five times at a diagonal, angled down the middle of the loaf and parallel to one another, slashing deeply and quickly, holding the blade at a ~30° angle.
17. Carefully slide the loaves and parchment off of the baking sheet or board and onto the heated baking stone. Slide the lower rack out a couple of inches and pour the 1 cup boiled water into the cast-iron skillet and immediately close the door. (The water will instantly turn to steam, so protect your arms and work quickly so it doesn’t escape.) Bake for 10 minutes, then open the oven briefly to let out any steam. (Some ovens hold steam better than others, so there may not be any left at that point.) Reduce the oven temperature to 450 degrees and bake until the loaves are deeply browned, another 10 to 15 minutes. Use a pair of tongs to remove the baguettes from the oven and let cool.

name: Pillowy Dinner Rolls (2 hr)
instructions:
1. Butter a 15-by-10-by-1-inch baking sheet.  In a small saucepan over medium heat, heat 4 T. butter, 1.5 C. milk and ¼ C. sugar until the mixture registers about 130 degrees on an instant-read thermometer.
2. In a stand mixer fitted with the paddle attachment, mix together 2¼ C. flour, 1 T. yeast and 1 t. salt on low speed just until combined. Add the hot milk mixture and mix on low speed until combined and smooth until a smooth, loose dough has formed, about 2 minutes. Cover the bowl and let the dough rest for 10 minutes.
3. With the mixer motor on low speed, add 2 eggs and mix until incorporated. Add another ¾ C. flour and continue to mix for about six minutes, until it becomes soft and pulls away from the sides of the bowl.
4. Shape the dough into smooth 13/4 in. balls. To encourage the best rise, try to form a "gluten cloak" by pulling the top of the dough down and around each ball before pinching and sealing around the bottom. Place the rolls on the prepared pan, spacing them about 3/4 inch apart in four rows of six. Cover with a clean kitchen towel and let rise for 45 minutes. The rolls will look puffy and will almost be touching, but most of the rising will take place during baking.
5. Position a rack in the middle of the oven and preheat to 375 degrees.  Bake for about 20 minutes, or until the rolls are touching and fill the pan; they will turn golden brown. Remove from the oven and brush with the melted butter. Using a spatula, slide the rolls onto a wire rack or serve directly from the pan, warm or at room temperature.

name: Yeast Rolls (serves six)
instructions:
1.  Boil 1 potato (sliced into 1” slices) for 10 minutes or until very tender.  Drain potato well, reserving 1 C. of cooking liquid and allow to cool.
2.  Mash potato with a fork or ricer.  Stir in ½ C. whole milk, 1 t. salt, 2 T. sugar and  2.5 T. melted butter (mixture will be lumpy).
3.  When potato water has cooled to “warm”, take ½ C. and stir in 1 pkg active dry yeast.  Allow to stand 5 minutes or longer until foamy.  Stir into potato mixture then add 2 2/3 C. flour, mixing thoroughly until a soft dough forms.
4.  Turn out dough onto a floured surface and knead ten minutes until dough is smooth and elastic.
5.  Brush a large bowl with melted butter and turn dough in to coat.  Cover tightly with plastic wrap and let rise, chilled, 8-12 hours.
6.  Punch down dough and cut in half.  Roll each half out into a 12”-long log on a lightly floured surface and cut into 12 1”-thick slices.  Roll each into a ball and loosely space onto a buttered 13”x9”x2” baking pan.  Cover with a moist cloth and allow to rise until double (1-2 hr.).
7.  Preheat oven to 375°F.  Baste rolls with melted butter and bake until golden brown – 25-30 minutes.  Loosen with a knife and transfer rolls to a rack to cool slightly.

name: Garlic and Black Olive Bread
instructions: 1.  Make bread as usual, adding in 6 cloves of crushed, roasted garlic, 1 T. orange zest, 1 t. red pepper flakes, and 3/4 C. chopped oil-cured olives before the final kneading.

name: Traditional Scones I
instructions:
1.  Preheat oven to 425° F.  Combine 2.5 C. flour, 1 T. baking powder, 1/2 t. salt and 1/4 C. sugar in a large bowl.  Cut in 8 T. cold butter with a fork or food processor until uniformly crumbly.
2.  Mix in 2/3 C. milk and mix into the dough.  Form into a bowl and knead 10-12 times.
3.  Cut dough in half.  Pat each half into a 6-in. circle on a lightly floured surface and cut into 1/6ths.  Place wedges on a cookie sheet, separated slightly, and bake 12 minutes, or until medium brown.

name: Traditional Scones II
instructions:
1.  Preheat oven to 450° F.  Combine 2.5 C. flour, 1 T. baking powder, 1/2 t. salt and 1/4 C. sugar in a large bowl.  Cut in 8 T. cold butter with a fork or food processor until uniformly crumbly.
2.  Mix in one egg and just enough heavy cream (approx. 2/3 C) to make the dough slightly sticky.  Turn onto a lightly floured surface and knead once or twice, then press into a ¾-in. think circle and cut into 2-in. rounds.  Place on ungreased cookie sheet, baste tops with cream and sprinkle with sugar.  Bake 9-11 minutes or until golden brown.

name: Buttermilk Biscuits (serves six)
instructions:
1.  3 C. flour, 1 T. baking powder, 3/4 t. cream of tartar, 1/2 t. salt, 1.5 T. sugar and 1 t. baking soda.  Cut in 1 C. shortening, then add 1.5 C. buttermilk and mix well.
2.  Knead briefly, then roll out, cut into biscuits, place on a well-greased baking sheet and bake at 425° F for 15-20 min.
[For scones, add two eggs beaten with 1 T. water and ½ C. currants]

name: Irish Soda Bread
instructions:
1.  Preheat oven to 350° F.  Grease an oven-proof skillet with butter then line with parchment or wax paper
2.  Whisk together 3. C. flour, 2/3 C. sugar, 1 T. baking powder, and 1 .5 t.   salt.  In a separate bowl, combine 1.75 C. buttermilk, 2 eggs (beaten), and 2 T. melted butter.  Combine wet and dry ingredients and stir until just combined.  Stir in 1.5 C. raisins and/or currants and 1 T. caraway seeds (optional).
3.  Pour batter into skillet and brush top with melted butter.  Bake until golden and firm to the touch, ~1 hour.  Cool 10 minutes, then serve with Cheddar cheese and tart apples.

name: Skillet Cornbread
instructions:
1.  Preheat oven to 375° F.  On the stovetop, melt 12 T. butter over medium heat in a cast iron skillet, swirling occasionally until the foam subsides and the butter is a deep nut brown (but do not burn).
2.  Pour butter into a large bowl and whisk in ½ C. maple syrup, then 2.25 C. buttermilk and finally 3 large eggs.  Mix in 1.5 C. yellow cornmeal, ½ C. whole wheat flour, ½ C. all-purpose flour, 1.5 T. baking powder, 1.5 t. kosher salt and ½ t. baking soda.
3.  Re-warm skillet, return the batter to it and bake until top is golden brown (30-40 min).  Remove from oven and allow to cool before slicing.

name: Jalapeño muffins
instructions:
1.  Preheat oven to 400° F.  Sauté 1 C. corn and 1 finely chopped jalapeño in 2 T. butter until soft.  Melt 4 T. butter in a separate pan.
2.   Mix together 1 C. flour, 1 C. cornmeal, 1.5 t. baking powder, 1.5 t. baking soda and 1 t. kosher salt.  Beat it two eggs and 1.5 C. buttermilk, then add the corn/jalapeño and 3 oz. grated cheddar cheese.  Mix well.
3.  Grease a muffin pan, then add batter, filling each well only ~3/4 full. Bake 15-20 minutes, or until golden brown.

name: Everything Bagels (1 hr 20 min, plus a day)
instructions:
1. In the bowl of a stand mixer fitted with a dough hook attachment, combine 5 C. bread flour, 4 t. barley malt syrup and 2 t. salt. Add 1.5 t. rapid-rise dry yeast and 1.5 C. water, then mix on the lowest speed until the dough starts to come together around the dough hook, about 4 minutes. Increase the speed to medium-low and knead until the dough is cohesive, smooth and stiff, 7 to 10 minutes.
2. Dust a large rimmed baking sheet with 3 T. cornmeal. Turn the dough out onto a work surface, then divide it into eight equal portions. Roll the pieces into smooth balls and cover with plastic wrap to rest at room temperature for 5 minutes.
3. Form each dough ball into an 11-inch-long rope by rolling it under your palms. Shape each rope into a circle with a diameter of about 4 inches, overlapping the ends by about 1 1/2 inches. Pinch the overlapped areas firmly together, moistening them lightly with water if the ends won't stick. Working with one at a time, place your fingers through each ring of dough; with the pinched-together seam facing down, roll the rope several times, applying firm pressure to seal the seam to form a bagel. Each ring should be about the same thickness all around; if it's not, you can roll it in other places around the ring to even it out.
4. Place the dough rings on the cornmeal-covered baking sheet as you work, spaced 1 to 2 inches apart. Cover tightly with plastic wrap and refrigerate overnight (12 to 18 hours). The rings will have puffed and spread slightly.
5. When ready to boil and bake the bagels, place a pizza stone or baking stone, if using, on the middle oven rack. Preheat the oven to 450 degrees. (The longer you can preheat, the better; up to 1 hour is great.)
6. Fill a large, wide pot with 3 inches of water and bring to a boil over high heat. Set a wire rack near your work space.  Gently slide three or four dough rings at a time into the boiling water, stirring and briefly submerging them with a metal skimmer or slotted spoon, until very slightly puffed, 30 to 35 seconds. Transfer the dough rings to the wire rack, bottom (flatter) sides down, to drain. Repeat with the remaining rings.
7. Combine 2 T. each of sesame and poppy seeds and 1 T. each caraway seeds, sea or kosher salt, dehydrated onion flakes and dehydrated garlic flakes.  Dip the rings in the mix and transfer to a baking sheet. Bake for 12 to 18 minutes, or until the bagels are a deep golden brown and crisp, rotating the baking sheet halfway through.
8. Using tongs, transfer the bagels to a wire rack to cool. Wait for a few minutes before splitting open the hot bagels.

name: Dutch Puff
instructions:
1.  Heat oven to 425°F.  Combine 1 C. flour, ½ t. kosher salt and ½ t. ground pepper and mix well.  In a separate bowl, whisk together 8 eggs and ¾ C. milk, then whisk into the dry ingredients.  Add 2 T. finely chopped thyme and 2 T. minced chives.
2.  Melt 6 T. butter in a 12” oven-proof skillet over medium-high heat.  When it has turned nutty, swirl around once or twice, then add batter and top with ¾ C. grated Parmigiano-Reggiano or Gruyére cheese.  Place in oven and bake 25 minutes.  Serve with lemon slices and sriracha sauce, alongside the soup of your choice.

name: Matafan
instructions:
1.  Pre-heat oven to 425°F.  Cook 4 oz. smoked bacon over medium heat, stirring often, until golden at the edges.  Remove from heat.
2.  Place 1.5 C. flour in a large bowl and fom a well in the center.  Crack 3 large eggs in the well and add 1. C. milk and a large pinch of salt.  Whisk egges together, slowly working in the flour and another 1 C. milk.  Stir in 1 bunch chives (minced) or 4 scallions (minced).
3.  Melt 3 T. unsalted butter in a cast-iron skillet over medium heat.  When foam has subsided, pour batter in and cook 4 minutes, until golden brown on the bottom.  Top with the bacon and bake in overn for 15-20 minutes, until buffed and golden.  When done, remove from oven sprinkle with 2 oz. Gruère cheese (grated) and serve immediately.

name: Potato Kugel
instructions:
1.  Heat oven to 350°F with a cast iron skillet inside.  Peel 3 lb. russet potatoes and place in a bowl of water.  Beat 3 eggs with 1 T. kosher salt and ¼ t. ground pepper in a large mixing bowl.  Using a food processor, grate 1 white onion, then the potatoes.  Add to egg mixture along with 6 T. vegetable oil, 1/3 C. flour and ¼ t. baking powder, and mix well.
2.  When oven is to temperature, remove the skillet and slick with vegetable oil.  Quickly add the potato mixture, smooth the top and return to the oven.  Bake 2 hr.

name: Bearnaise Sauce
instructions:
1. In a small non-reactive saucepan, combine 2 T.  tarragon, 2 shallots (minced),  ¼ C. white wine vinegar and  ¼ C. white wine over medium-high heat.  Season with white pepper, salt and a dash of cayenne.  Bring to a simmer and cook until reduced by half. Remove from heat and set aside to cool.
2.  Melt 1 stick butter in a non-reactive saucepan.  Blend 3 egg yolks into the cooled bearnaise reduction. When the butter is melted, add the bearnaise reduction and stir vigorously over low heat until the mixture has thickened slightly.

name: Aïoli (serves 12)
instructions:
1.  Mince 6-8 cloves of garlic and press with 1/2 t. kosher salt until smooth.
2.  Add 3 egg yolks and blend well.  SLOWLY blend in 1.5 C. olive oil with constant beating to make an emulsion.  When all the oil is added, add 1 T. lemon juice.  Serve with fish or shellfish, or on toast.

name: Saffron-Orange Aïoli
instructions:
Stir 1/8 t. crumbled saffron threads with 1 T. hot water in a small bowl.
Transfer to a food processor with 1/4 C. fresh orange juice, 1 T. fresh lemon juice, 3/4 t. salt, 1/4 t. black pepper and 1 egg yolk.  Pulse until mixed well.
With motor running, add 3/4 C. olive oil in a thin steady stream.  Make sure the oil emulsifies as it is added.  Transfer to a bowl, cover and chill until used.

name: Sauce Marnay (serves six)
instructions:
1.  Melt 2 t. butter with 1.5 t. flour.  Cook for 2-3 min, then add 1/8 t. cayenne, 1/8 t. nutmeg, salt and pepper to taste.
2.  Remove from heat and add 1/2 C. grated Gruyere cheese.  Stir until melted.

name: Romesco Sauce
instructions:
1.  Halve and seed 1 ancho chile.  Submerge in boiling water, remove from heat and allow to hydrate 30 minutes (or alternatively, roast , peel and seed one poblano chile).
2. Toast ½ C. each almonds and hazelnuts (or pine nuts pecans).  Heat 4 T. olive oil in skillet, then add nuts and 4-8 cloves of garlic (peeled and sliced).  Cook with stirring until browned, then add 2 (jarred) roasted red peppers (drained and chopped).
3.  Seed the ancho chile and chop up.  Add to skillet, along with one tomato (chopped and seeded).  Stir for a minute or so, then remove from heat and allow to cool.
4.  Blend to a rough paste, then with additional blending, add 2/3 C. dry white wine, 1 T. sherry vinegar, ½ t. paprika and salt to taste.

name: Mustard-Shallot Vinaigrette (serves six)
instructions: 1.  Place 1 shallot (minced) with 2 T. white wine vinegar and 1 T. dijon mustard in a glass jar, then shake well.  Add ¼ t. salt and ½ t. ground pepper and shake again.  Add 1 C. olive oil, 1/3 C. at a time and shaking well after each addition.

name: Toni’s Mud Cake
instructions:
1. Cream 2 C. sugar with 2 sticks of butter.  Mix in 1.5 C. flour, 1 t. vanilla, 1/2 t. salt, 4 eggs and 1/3 C. cocoa.  Cook 30 min. at 350° F.
2.  For icing, melt 2/3 C. butter and add 1/3 C. cocoa and 1/2 C. dry milk in a little water.  Heat thoroughly, then add powdered sugar until thick.
3.  When the cake is cool, top with icing and serve.

name: Marietta’s Cheesecake
instructions:
1.  Mix 1/2 stick melted butter with 1.5 C. Graham cracker crumbs.  Press into a 9-in. pie plate and bake 5 min. at 350° F.
2.  Cream 9 oz. cream cheese with 1/2 C. sugar.  Add 2 eggs, 1/2 t. vanilla and mix well. Pour into crust and bake 20 min. at 350° F.  remove and allow to cool.
3.  Mix 12 oz. sour cream with 3 T. sugar, 1/2 t. cinnamon and 1/2 t. vanilla.  Spread on top of first layer and bake 5 min. at 325° F.  Chill at least 2 hr. before serving.

name: Mel’s Cheesecake
instructions:
1.  Mix  1.5 C. Graham cracker crumbs with 1/2 stick of melted butter.  Press into a  9-in. springform pan and bake 5 min. at 350° F.
2.  Mix 1 pkg. cream cheese, 1 can sweetented condensed milk and 1/4 C. lemon juice.  Pour into the pie crust and chill.
3.  Strain juice from a can of blueberries, thicken with 2 T. cornstarch until thickened like pudding.  Add the berries and top the pie.  Refrigerate before serving.

name: Russ’s Cheesecake
instructions:
1.  Mix 1/2 stick melted, unsalted butter with 2 C. Graham cracker crumbs, 3 T. sugar and a pinch of salt  Press into a buttered springform pan and bake 10 min. at 350° F.
2.  Cream 2 lb. cream cheese in a blender until soft and creamy (10 minutes or longer).  Add 1 1/3 C. sugar and 1 t. salt, then continue beating another 5 min.  Slowly add 2 t. vanilla extract and 4 large eggs (blend at least 1 minute between each egg). Pour into crust (the batter should come close to the top, but with ~1/2 inch for the second layer).
3.  Place pan in a roaster or large oven-proof skillet and fill with boiling water.  Bake the cheesecake for 1 hour 30 minutes.  Turn off the oven’s heat and prop the oven door open with a wooden spoon. Allow the cheesecake to cool in its water bath for another hour.
4.  Mix 16 oz. sour cream with 4 T. sugar, 1/3 t. cinnamon and 1/3 t. vanilla.  Spread on top of cheesecake and bake 10 min. at 325° F.  Chill at least 4 hr. and preferably overnight.

name: Tina’s Pecan Pie (serves two)
instructions:
1.  In a food processor (or by hand) mix 2.5 C. flour with 6 T. chilled vegetable shortening and 6 T. chilled, unsalted butter until the mixture looks like coarse meal.
2.  With the motor running, add 6-7 T. ice water.  Process until the dough pulls away from the sides of the processor.  Divide in two, flatten into disks between wax paper or plastic wrap, and refrigerate at least 1 hr.
3.  Roll out half the pie dough into a round and place in a 9-in. pie plate.
4.  Beat 3 eggs, then add 2/3 C. sugar, 1/3 t. salt, 1/3 C. melted butter and 1 C. dark Karo syrup.  Mix well, then add 1 C. pecan halves.
5.  Pour into the pie crust and bake 45 min at 375° F.

name: Savory Apple Tart
instructions:
1.  Mix 1 packet yeast and 1 t. sugar with ¾ C. warm water in a small bowl and let stand for five minutes.
2.  In a separate large bowl, whisk together 1.5 C. all-purpose flour, ½ C. whole wheat flour, ½ C. cornmeal and 2 t. sea salt.  Stir yeast in yeasst mixture and 1/3 C. olive oil.  Turn out on a floured surface and knead until smooth and elastic.  Transfer to a lightly oiled bowl, cover loosely with plastic wrap and allow to rest until doubled.
3.  Pre-heat oven to 425° F.  Toss together 1.75 lb. Granny Smith apples, ¾ lb. sliced shallots, ¼ C. olive oil, ½ t. sea salt ¼ t. black pepper and 5 sprigs of thyme.  Spread onto two large baking sheets and roast, tossing occasionally ~30 min. until golden and tender.
4.  Increase oven temperature to 450° F.  Lightly oil a large, rimmed baking sheet then punch down dough and roll or stretch out on the baking sheet.  Scatter apple-shallot mixture over dough, then do the same with ¾ C. finely crumbled blue cheese.  Drizzle with olive oil and top with flaky sea salt and freshly ground black pepper.  Bake ~20 min. until crust is golden and cheese just melted.  Allow to cool then cut into squares and serve.

name: Ultimate Brownies (time 1 hr)
instructions:
1. Position a rack in the middle of the oven and preheat to 350 degrees. Spray a 9-by-13-inch pan with nonstick cooking oil spray. Line the bottom with a piece of parchment paper long enough to have an overhang over two opposite sides of the pan.
2. In a medium saucepan over medium heat, melt 3 sticks (12 oz.) butter. Remove from the heat and add the 2 C. cocoa powder, stirring to combine. Let cool slightly.
3. In a large bowl, whisk 6 large eggs together, then add 2 C. granular sugar, 2 C. light brown sugar and 2 T. vanilla extract, stirring to combine. Add the cooled butter-chocolate mixture, then fold in 2 C. all-purpose flour and ½ t. salt until just combined; do not overmix. Spread the batter evenly in the pan, making sure the corners are filled.
4. Bake for 40 to 45 minutes, or until a crust forms on the surface and a toothpick inserted into the center comes out mostly clean. Transfer to a wire rack and let cool completely in the pan, about 2 hours. Using the parchment, lift the brownie out of the pan (if you meet any resistance, gently run a butter knife around the edges.) Slice into 24 pieces (or however many you like) and serve.

name: Texas Sheet Cake
instructions:
1. Sift together 2 C. sugar and 2 C. flour in a large bowl; set aside.
2. Place ½ C. butter, ½ C. shortening and ¼ C. unsweetened cocoa and 1 C. water in a medium saucepan over medium-high heat. Bring to a boil, stirring constantly, until butter and shortening melt. Remove from heat, and pour over sugar/flour mixture and mix well. Allow to cool slightly.
3.  Stir in ½ C. buttermilk, 2 large eggs (slightly beaten), 1 t. baking soda and 1 t. vanilla. Pour into a greased and lightly floured 15- x 10-inch jellyroll pan.
4.  Bake at 400° for 20 minutes. (cake will have a fudgelike texture.) Spread warm cake with chocolate Icing.

name: Chocolate Icing
instructions:
1. Combine ½ C. butter, ¼ C. unsweetened cocoa, and 1/3 C. milk in medium saucepan. Cook over low heat 5 minutes or until butter melts.
2. Bring to a boil then remove from heat, and stir in 16 oz. powdered sugar, 1 t. vanilla and 1 C. chopped pecans. Beat at medium speed with an electric mixer until smooth and sugar dissolves.

name: Wassail
instructions:
1.  Pre-heat oven to 350°F.  Core 5-6 medium crips apples (Honey Crisp, Fujio or McIntosh) and place in a glass baking dish.  Mix ½ C. light brown sugar and ½ C. dark brown sugar, and spoon into apples.  Add 1 C. water to dish and bake ~ 1 hr. until tender.
2.  Place 2 C. Madeira, two 22.4 oz. bottles of ale, 4 bottles of Strongbow cider and 1 C. apple cider in a large pot.  Place 12 whole cloves, 12 whole allspice berries, two 2-in. sticks of cinnamon and the zest from one orange in cheesecloth and add to liquid.  Season with 1 t. ground ginger and 1 t. ground nutmeg.  Simmer for at least 1 hr.
3.   Add liquid from the apple dish to the cider, then the apples.  Serve in heatproof cups.

name: Tom and Jerry (for 12)
instructions:
1.  Separate 6 eggs.  Beat yolks until thin as water, gradually adding 1 lb. sugar, 1 oz. añejo rum, 3 T. vanilla extract, ¾ t. ground cinnamon, ¼ t. ground allspice, ¼ t. ground nutmeg, ¼ t. ground cloves and two dashes of Angostura bitters.
2.  In a separate bowl, beat eggwhites until stiff, then fold into yolk mixture.
3.  Bring 3 qt. whole milk to a boil.  Then to 10-oz. warmed glasses add: 2 oz. of the batter, 1 oz. añejo rum (12 oz. total) and 1 oz. cognac (12 oz. total).  Top off with boiling milk while stirring briskly.  Dust with ground nutmeg and serve.

name: Kathy’s Tenured Cider (serves two)
instructions:
1.  Simmer all day:
2 qts. apple cider
1/2 t. cinnamon
1/2 t. mint leaves
1/4 t. nutmeg
1/2 C. powdered sugar
2-3 sliced oranges with cloves inserted
2.  Add 1/2 -1 C. brandy and serve warm.

name: Mom’s Egg Scramble (serves 8)
instructions:
1.  Sauté 1/4 C. chopped green onion and 1 C. diced Canadian bacon (or other breakfast meat) in 3 T. butter in a large pan.  Add 12 eggs and scramble until just set.
2.  Melt 2 T. butter and add 2 T. flour, 1/2 t. salt and 1/8 t. pepper, then cook 5 min.  Add 2 C. milk with continuous stirring and mix until smooth.  Finally, mix in 1 C. grated cheddar cheese and cook until melted.
3.  Fold the cooked eggs and 1/2 C. chopped mushrooms into the cheese sauce.  Pour into a 12-in. x 7-in. baking dish and top with buttered crumbs and paprika.  Bake uncovered at 350° F for 30 min.

name: Yorkshire Pancakes
instructions:
Preheat oven to 425°F.  Lightly beat 2 eggs in a bowl, then add 1/2 C. flour, 1/2 C. milk and a pinch of nutmeg.  Lightly beat until blended but still slightly lumpy.  Refrigerate at least one hour.
Remove batter from refrigerator.  Melt 4 T. butter in a 12-inch skillet.  When very hot (but not browned) pour in the batter and bake in oven until billowing and golden brown (~15 minutes).  Remove from oven, sprinkle with confectioner’s sugar and return to oven another 1-2 minutes.  Remove, sprinkle with juice from half a lemon and serve with jams or marmalade.

name: Waffles
instructions:
1. Grease and preheat waffle iron according to manufacturer's directions.
2. In a medium bowl whisk together 2 C flour (regular and/or whole wheat), ½ t. baking soda, 1 t. baking powder, 1 t. salt, and 3 T. sugar. In another bowl beat together 3 eggs and 2 T. melted butter, then add 2 C. buttermilk. Add the wet ingredients to the dry and stir until combined. Allow to rest for 5 minutes.
3. Ladle the recommended amount of waffle batter onto the iron according to the manufacturer's recommendations. Close iron top and cook until the waffle is golden on both sides and is easily removed from iron. Serve immediately or keep warm in a 200 degree F oven until ready to serve.

name: Russ’ Better Tortilla Española (serves six)
instructions:
1.  Add 2.5 C. of potatoes (peeled and diced), 1.5 C. chopped onion, 1 chopped red pepper and 2 chopped jalapeños with a little salt to 1.5 C. hot oil over medium-high heat.  Lower heat and cook vegetables until tender but not colored (~45 min.).  Drain vegetables in a large collander over a bowl and allow to cool 5 min.
2.  Lightly beat 10 large eggs and stir in vegetables with 1 t. vegetable oil, salt and pepper to taste.  Return 1 T. of oil to the pan and then add the egg mixture, pressing the potatoes flush with the eggs.  Cook over low heat for 15 min. or until almost set.  Invert tortilla onto a large flat plate and slide back into skillet cooked side up.  Cook another 15 min. until set.  Serve warm.

name: Russ’s Legendary Cinnamon Rolls (serves six)
instructions:
1.  Heat 1/3 C. milk to just boiling and remove from heat.  Add ¼ C. sugar and ¼ C. butter.  Allow to melt, then cool.
2.  Mix 1 pkg yeast in ¼ C. warm water.  Stir in above.  Add 1 egg and 2 C. flour, mix well then add another ½ C. flour.  Knead well, then let rise in a greased bowl for one hour.
3.  Cream ¼ C. butter with ¼ C. brown sugar.  Spread in pan and sprinkle with ½ C. pecan halves.
4.  Roll dough into a 16” x 12” rectangle.  Sprinkle with ½ C. raisins (or currants), ½ C. brown sugar and ½ t. cinnamon.  Roll into a log, cut into 12 circles, place in pan and cover with a moist towel.  Let rise 1 hr., then bake at 375°F for 30 min.

name: Standard Waffles
instructions:
1. Grease and preheat waffle iron according to manufacturer's directions.
2. In a medium bowl whisk together 2 C flour (regular and/or whole wheat), ½ t. baking soda, 1 t. baking powder, 1 t. salt, and 3 T. sugar.  In another bowl beat together 3 eggs and 2 T. melted butter, then add 2 C. buttermilk. Add the wet ingredients to the dry and stir until combined. Allow to rest for 5 minutes.
3. Ladle the recommended amount of waffle batter onto the iron according to the manufacturer's recommendations. Close iron top and cook until the waffle is golden on both sides and is easily removed from iron. Serve immediately or keep warm in a 200 degree F oven until ready to serve.

name: Christmas Casserole (serves 8)
instructions:
1.  Toast 6-8 english muffins (or croissants).  Crumble into pieces when cooled.
2.  Sauté one bunch of scallions (sliced, including light green part) and 1 lb. sweet Italian sausage (removed from casing) in 1 t. olive oil, breaking up the meat as it browns.  Toward the end, add 2 t. chopped sage and cook a final 5 minutes.  Drain and allow to cool, then toss with toasted bread and spread in a lightly oiled 9”x13” casserole.
3.  Whisk together 8 large eggs, 3 C. whole milk, 1 C. heavy cream, 8 oz. grated Gruyére, salt  and pepper to taste.  Pour over sausage and bread in the casserole, cover with plastic wrap and refrigerate overnight.
4.  Preheat oven to 350°F.  Remove casserole from refrigerator and top with grated parmesano reggiano.  Bake 45 min., then allow to cool 10 min.  top with scallion greens and serve.

name: Graham’s Butter Chicken (serves four)
instructions:
1. Marinade 1 lb. chicken (cut into small pieces) in ½ C. full fat yogurt overnight.
2. Heat 1 T. canola oil in a large non-stick pan over high heat.  Add the chicken and yogurt, cook for about 1 min. until sauce begins to bubble, then reduce heat.
3.  Stir, then add  2 diced tomatoes and the following (with stirring): 1 t. dried fenugreek leaves, ¼ t. ground fenugreek seeds, 1 t. turmeric, 1 t. cumin, 2 t. paprika, 1 t. cayenne, 1 pinch ground cloves, 1 t. white sugar.
4.  Add ~½ C. water, then 4 T. Patak’s butter curry paste (or 2 T. tomato paste, 2 cloves strong garlic, 1 t. tamarind paste and 1 t. lime juice).  Cook on medium-low with occasional stirring until chicken is done (approx 20-30 min.)  Add some of the water if it starts to become too dry.  Add salt to taste.
5. Mix in ½ can coconut milk, 4 T. butter and 4 T. heavy cream.  Return to boil briefly, then serve (with rice, naan or whatever).

name: Tess’ crab cakes (makes 8-12)
instructions:
1.  Gently combine 1 lb. lump crab meat, 1/2 C. diced red bell pepper, 1 minced, seeded jalapeño, 4 t. old bay seasoning, 2 cloves of garlic (minced), 4-6 T. mayonnaise, 1 C. fresh breadcrumbs and salt, pepper, cayenne, white pepper, thyme and chopped onion to taste.  Shape into cakes and chill 1 hr.
2.  Dip each cake in 2 eggs (slightly beaten), then 1 C. breadcrumbs and set aside.  Sauté cakes a couple at a time in 1 T. (each) oil until browned.  Serve with spicey mayonnaise (below).
3.  Combine 2 C. mayonnaise, 6 T. hot chile sauce and 2 T. lemon juice.  Mix well and chill, then serve with crab cakes.

name: Rob’s **** Beef and Broccoli Stir-Fry (serves six)
instructions:
1.  Marinate 2 lb. beef strips (thinly sliced) for 1 hr. in 3 T. soy sauce, 2 T. dry white wine, 2 T. cornstarch, 1 t. sugar and 1/4 t. pepper.
2.  Trim and cut into segments one bunch of broccoli, along with four carrots (cut into thin slices length-wise) and 1/2 onion, cut into wedges (optional).
3.  Heat 8 T. peanut (or vegetable) oil in a wok or heavy skillet over high heat.  When very hot, add the beef strips and cook until browned.  Add the vegetables and cook until done (~5 min).  Add the marinade, 4 T. soy sauce, 4 T. cornstarch and 3 T. peanut butter and stir until well mixed and thickened.  Serve over rice.

name: Vietnamese pho (soup) (serves six)
instructions:
1.  Place 2 lb. oxtails, 3 lb. beef bones (shin or neck) and 1/2 t. salt in 14 C. water and slowly bring to a boil.
2.  Char one halved onion and a 3-inch piece of ginger (halved) in a dry skillet, cut sides down, over high heat.  Turn and char skin sides, then add to stock.
3.  When stock begins to boil, reduce heat and simmer uncovered, skimming off foam that comes to the top.  Continue for 30 min. or longer.
4.  Add 8 whole star anises, 2 cinnamon sticks, 2 large bay leaves, 5 whole cloves, and 1 T. sugar.  Place 2 t. fennel seeds in a tea ball (or tied in cheese cloth)and add to the stock.  Simmer another 3-4 hours.  Strain and pass through a fine sieve.  Refrigerate 3-4 hr. and skim off any remaining fat.
5.  Place 1 lb. rice vermicelli in a large bowl and cover with cold water.  Soak until pliable (about 30 min.) and drain.
6.  Take the stock from the refrigerator and bring to a boil.  Place a handful of vermicelli into a strainer that will fit in the pot, and dip the strainer into the hot stock.  Swirl intil the vermicelli is tender but still a little chewy (about 20 sec).  Shake vermicelli dry over the stock and place in a soup bowl.  Repeat with the remaining noodles.
7.  Divide 3/4 very thinly sliced lean beef, 1/2 C. mung beans, 1/2 C. cilantro leaves, 1/2 C. chopped basil leaves and 2 chopped scallions among the six bowls.  Ladle the very hot stock over the contents.  Squeeze some lemon juice on top and add pepper to taste.  Serve with 3 chopped Thai chiles and bottled fish sauce.

name: Russ’s Tteokbokki
instructions:
1.  Thinly slice 8 oz. sirloin and marinate in 1 t. soy sauce and 2 t. sesame oil with 2 minced cloves of garlic.
2.   Boil 8 oz. (1 pkg) tteok rice cakes in water according to package instructions.  Drain and reserve.
3.  Cut 1 bunch scallions into 2-inch sections, including green parts, and thinly slice 1 small onion.
4.  Cut 2 C. green cabbage (or 1 C. each cabbage and radicchio) crosswise into large pieces.
5.  Set a large skillet over high heat.  When hot, add meat and stir-fry for 1-2 min until browned, adding 1 tsp gochungjang paste toward the end.  Place in a large bowl and keep warm.
6.  Clean the skillet, then add the onion, scallions and cabbage, and cook an additional 2-3 minutes until crispy.
7.  Add tteok, 1-2 T. gochujang paste, 2 t. sugar and 1/3 C. water, then stir and simmer until thickened.
8.  Top with sesame seeds and serve.

name: Bernie Choong’s Curried Chicken (serves four)
instructions:
1.  Sauté 1/2 chopped onion in 1 T. oil until clear.  Mix a little water with 2-3 T. curry powder and make into a paste.  Add to the onion and sauté briefly.
2.  Blanch four chicken breasts in boiling water, then simmer in milk for ~ 10 min.  Add 1.5 lb. new potatoes (quartered), salt and pepper to taste.  Cover and bake at 350° F until done (~ 30 min).

name: Hoisin sauce
instructions:
1.  Combine ¼ cup soy sauce (preferably dark), 3 T. molasses (or honey), 2 t. sesame oil, 1 T. rice vinegar, 2 T. peanut butter, 2 garlic cloves (finely minced), 2 T. sriracha sauce and  ¼ t. ground pepper in a sauce pan over medium heat.
2. Combine 1 t. cornstarch with 1 T. water and add to the above.  Stir well until the desired thickness is achieved.

name: General Tso’s Chicken (serves 4)
instructions:
In a small bowl, combine 4 T. tomato paste, 1 t. potato flour, 1 t. dark soy sauce, 3 t. light soy sauce, 3 T. rice vinegar and 9 T. chicken stock.  Set aside.
Skin, debone and remove as much of thesinew as possible from 24 oz. chicken thighs (8-10).  Cut in half horizontally if too thick, lightly score with crosshatches then slice into 1/4-in. slices lengthwise.
Mix 1 t. dark soy sauce, 4 t. light soy sauce and 2 egg yolks.  Add 4 T. potato flour and 4 t. peanut oil, and mix well.  Snip 15-20 dried red chiles into 1/4-in. pieces with scissors, discard the seeds and add to mixture.
Pour 3.5 C. peanut oil into a large wok, enough to be 1.5 in. deep.  Set over high heat until oil is 350-400°F, then fry the chicken in 3 or 4 batches until crisp and deep-golden in color (3-4 minutes).  Transfer the chicken to a plate with a slotted spoon.
Pour off the oil (saving if desired), wipe out the wok and return to heat.  Add 4 T. peanut oil and allow to heat well.  Add the chilies and stir-fry for a few seconds (till they just begin to change color, then add 4 T. finely chopped ginger and 4 t. minced garlic, and cookk a few seconds longer (until fragrant).  Return the chicken to the wok and stir vigorously to coat.  Remove to a serving bowl, mix in the sauce and top with chopped scallions.  Serve with rice.

name: Fettuccine with Spring Vegetables
instructions:
1.  Cook 3/4 lb. fettuccine according to the instructions on the package.
2.  Snap off the woody ends to 3/4 lb. asparagus and cut diagonally into 2 in. lengths.  Slice the white part of one small leek (rinsing off any grit).  Sauté the asparagus and leek in 2 T. olive oil for 5 min (until just softened).  Add 2 minced cloves of garlic and 2 C. frozen peas and cook until just tender (2-3 min).
3.  Drain the fettuccine, return to the pan and add the vegetable mixture, along with 4 T. olive oil, 1/2 C. freshly grated Parmesan cheese, salt and coarsely ground pepper to taste.  Toss well and serve.

name: Gratin of Penne and vegetables
instructions:
1.  Boil 1/2 lb. penne (macaroni or elbow spaghetti)  for 8 min.  Drain and rinse.
2.  Bring 1/3 C. water to a boil in a saucepan.  Add 1 lb. broccoli flowerlets in 1.5-in. pieces and cook 3 min.  Add 8 oz. mushrooms, 1 diced red pepper, 1/2 C. walnuts, 2 t. olive oil and mix well.  Add 10 oz. rinsed, shredded spinach and cook 2-3 min.
3.  Melt 1 T. unsalted butter in a saucepan and whisk in 1.5 T. flour.  Stir in 2 C. milk and bring to a boil with constant whisking.  Add 1 C. heavy cream, salt/pepper to taste and return to a boil.  Set aside.
4.  When ready to serve, mix the pasta and vegetables with 1 C. grated Swiss cheese and spread in a gratin dish.  Heat the mixture to bubbling in a 400°F oven, then broil for 5 min. to brown.  Serve at once.

name: Lasagna (serves six)
instructions:
1.  Simmer 2 cans tomato sauce, 1 t. sugar, 1 t. oregano, 1 t. salt, 1/4 t. pepper.
2.  In a separate pan, sauté one chopped onion and 1 clove crushed garlic.  Add 1 lb. ground beef and brown.  Drain and add to the tomato sauce.
3.  Cook 1 package of lasagna noodles per the instructions on the box.
4.  Grate 1 lb. mozzarella cheese.  Layer into a greased casserole dish: the sauce, noodles, grated mozzarella, cottage cheese, noodles, sauce, grated mozzarella, cottage cheese, noodles.  Top with grated romano cheese.
5.  Bake at 375° F for 30 min.

name: Pesto
instructions:
1.  Purée 1 C. fresh basil, 4 cloves garlic, 1/2 C. pine nuts and 1 C. grated Romano cheese in a food processer.
2.  Slowly add 1 C. olive oil, pulsing the processor after each addition.  Serve over pasta.

name: Ragú (serves six)
instructions:
1.  Sauté one chopped onion, carrot and celery stalk in 3 T. butter, 2.5 T. olive oil and 6 strips of bacon.  When soft., add 3/4 C. pork, 3/4 C. beef, 1/4 C. sausage and 2-3 chopped chicken livers.  Add 1/3 C. dry white wine and cook down.
2.  Combine 4 T. tomato paste and 1.25 C. beef buillon, then add to the stew and cook 1-2 hr.  Add 4 T. milk.
3.  Sauté 1/4 lb. mushrooms in 3 T. butter with one clove of garlic and 1/4 C. chopped parsley.  Add to sauce at the last moment, and serve immediately over spaghetti.

name: Super Spaghetti (serves eight)
instructions:
1.  Grind 6 slices uncured bacon with pulsing in a food blender.  Add 2 crushed cloves of garlic, then gently mix in 2 lb. ground beef, 2/3 C. chopped roasted peppers from jar, 2/3 C. Japanese panko, 2 large eggs, ½ C. minced onion, ½ C. grated Parmesan cheese, 1 T. fresh marjoram, 2 T. crushed red pepper, ½ t. kosher salt and ½ t. black pepper.  Let stand 15 minutes.
2.  Puree 2 28-oz cans diced tomatoes (San Marzano, with juice) with 2 large cloves of garlic in a blender.
3.  Saute 6 slices uncured bacon, sliced into strips.  Add bacon strips to sauce, and brown meatballs in fat (with olive oil added) over medium heat.  Remove meatballs and sauté 3 C. chopped onions over medium high heat with 1.5 t. crushed red pepper until golden.  Add 2 C. dry white wine and simmer until reduced by half, then add tomato puree and meatballs.  Simmer until thickened.
4.  Prepare spaghetti per package instructions.  Toss with 2 T. olive oil and 1.5 T. minced fresh marjoram.  Serve with spaghetti and meatballs.

name: Spaghetti Marinara (serves eight as a side)
instructions:
1.  Place ¼ C. good olive oil in a large skillet over medium-high heat.  Slice 2 jalapeños lengthwise and remove stem and seeds.  Place in the oil and cook 5 minutes.  Add 2 cloves crushed garlic and cook another 3 minutes (do not let the garlic brown).  Remove and discard the jalapeños, but not the garlic.
2.  Open a 24-oz. can of San Marzano tomatoes into a large bowl and crush the tomatoes with your hand.  Rinse the can with 1 C. water and add to the tomatoes, then add the sauce to the skillet.
3.  Press a generous sprig of fresh basil (several leaves) into the tomato sauce and sprinkle with chopped fresh marjoram.  Cook for ~15 minutes until the sauce has thickened and the oil on top turned a deep orange.
4.   Prepare a 1 lb. package of pasta (spaghetti or linguini) per the package instructions for al dente.  When the pasta is cooked, strain and return to the pot, stir in the tomato sauce and cook another 1-2 minutes until done.  Top with pepper flakes.

name: Pizza (makes two 12” pies)
instructions:
1.  Mix 1 C. all-purpose flour with 1 C. 00 (finely ground) flour and 1 t. sea salt.  In a separate bowl, mix ¾ t. yeast with 1. C warm water and a pinch of sugar.  After a few minutes, add 1 t. olive oil and add to the flour mixture.
2.  Knead the dough for 3 minutes, then let rest for 15 minutes.  Knead for another 3 minutes, then cut in two and let rest, covered, for 2-3 hours (or refrigerate for as long as a week – just let it warm to room temperature before proceeding).
3.  While the dough is resting, purée 1 24-oz. can of San Marzano tomatoes with 2 T. olive oil and ½ t. salt.
3.  Place a pizza stone in the oven and pre-heat to 550 °F (this will take an hour or so).  When the oven is ready, coat the dough with flour on a heavily floured surface.  Gently but rapidly pat flat w ith your hands, holding the dough vertically and letting gravity stretch it (you will need to rotate the dough slowly as you pat it so as to make a round).
4.  Place the flattened dough on the back of a cookie sheet that has been well-floured, and gently work around the edge to make a small rim.  For pizza Margherita, place 3-4 spoonfuls of tomato sauce in the center of the flattened dough and spread with the back of the spoon (refrigerate te hrest of the sauce for another day).  Drizzle with a little olive oil, then add 4-5 slices of fresh mozzarella, alternating with fresh basil leaves.  (Alternatively, you can make a cheese pie by spreading 4 t. heavy cream on the stretched out dough and topping with any combination of fresh mozzarella, taleggio, chevre and/or parmesan cheeses.)  Using a spatula or pizza peel, slide the pizza onto the heated stone in the oven, using the spatula to spread the pie out if necessary.  IT DOES NOT HAVE TO BE PERFECTLY ROUND!  Bake 5-10 minutes, until the edge is slightly charred and the sauce bubbling, and serve immediately.

name: Jeff Master’s four-star chile verde (serves six)
instructions:
1.  Burn the skins off 2 doz. anaheim chiles.  Blend with six tomatillos.
2.  Sauté 2 spanish onions, 6 cloves of garlic , 16 jalapeños and (optional) 2 habaneros or serrenos chiles in 4 T. olive oil.
3.  Grill and cube 4 lb. pork.
4.  Add all the above to a large pot with 24 oz. chicken broth, and simmer several hours.

name: Open and reserve both the meat and the juice to 8 dozen littleneck or cherrystone clams.
instructions: Combine clams with 6 T. finely chopped onions, 3 t. finely chopped garlic, 3 T. chopped jalapeños (with seeds), 6 T. lime juice, 6 T. olive oil and 3/4 C. coriander (fresh, chopped).

name: Black Bean Muñeta (serves four)
instructions:
1. Simmer 1/2 lb. dried black beans (soaked in water overnight and drained) in 2 C. water with 1 bay leaf for 1.5 hr (add water as neded to keep the beans barely covered).
2.  Sauté 1 diced onion in 2 t. olive oil over medium heat for 5 min.  Add 4 minced cloves of garlic and cook another 1 min., then stir in 1/2 t. ground cumin and 2 T. chopped fresh oregano; cook another few minutes, then remove from heat.
3.  When the beans are cooked, stir in the onion mixture, 1 t. salt and pepper to taste.  Cook another 5 min, then cool and purée in a food processor.  Scrape into a serving bowl and garnish with sprigs of cilantro.

name: Steve’s Atomic Green Chile Enchiladas (serves six)
instructions:
1.  Peel and dice 4 avocados.  Add 8 oz. green chile sauce, 8 oz. of sour cream and warm on the stove.
2.  Broil four chicken breasts.  Allow to cool, then shred the meat.  Grate 1 lb. Monterey jack cheese.
3.  Warm a package of tortillas individually, cover each with sauce, add meat and cheese, and roll up.  Place in a greased casserole and cover with the remainder of the sauce.  Bake 15 min. at 325° F until the cheese is melted.  Serve with sour cream.

name: Russ' Chili
instructions:
1.  Boil 2 lb. lean ground beef in water to cover with 4 T. chile powder.  Drain & blot dry.
2.  Sauté one finely chopped red onion, 4 cloves chopped garlic, 4 jalapeños, (1 chopped habanero), 1 chopped bell pepper, 4 T. chile powder and 2 T. curry mix.
3.  Add one can stewed tomatoes,  one can tomato sauce, one can kidney beans and one can black beans.  Simmer at least four hours. (Try chicken broth instead and another can of stewed tomatoes instead of the tomato sauce).

name: Chile Manifesto
instructions:
Every practiced American cook’s recipe for chili is definitive and the best. A classic chili, the Texas bowl of red, is simply beef and hot peppers, simmered to perfection. But there’s no one recipe for the dish and no incorrect one — at least if we’re being honest with ourselves. You can make chili with beans; lamb or bison; venison, turkey or pork; with tomatoes or beer, with fresh chiles or dried, with chili powder or without. You should make chili as you like: delicious. We’ll help.  First, the ingredients:
Protein.  A great chili rests on two foundations: its protein, and the peppers that flavor it. It is, essentially, a stew. We’ll get to the chiles, but let’s begin with the protein. If you’re cooking with meat, look for a cut high in fat and flavor. If you’re cooking with beans, find a sturdy variety: A pinto or navy bean is excellent.
Beef, Pork and Lamb  –  Chuck beef, from the steer’s shoulder, is great for chili. But you can also do very well with brisket and short ribs, and there are fantastic chilis made of lamb and pork shoulder. Whatever protein you use, cut the meat into 2-inch cubes, or, if you’d like to work faster or simply prefer the texture, use ground meat.  In much of Texas and at the butcher shop anywhere, you can get your meat coarsely ground, which just about splits the difference between cubes and ground. But you can also use a combination: Some cooks even like to use a number of different cuts, combining stew meat with ground. Consider ¼ to ⅓ of a pound per person. It should yield enough fat to flavor your chili well.  Whatever you choose, be sure to fry some bacon in the pot before you get started, and then set it aside to crumble into the chili later in the process.
Poultry  –  There are those who swear by ground turkey chilis or who make the dish with chicken. Be careful when doing so, however, so that the meat does not dry out. Consider ¼ to ⅓ of a pound per person, supplemented perhaps with a few strips of bacon to help keep everything juicy. Or use chunks of dark meat from the richer, fattier thighs, or even duck.
Game  –  Farm-raised or wild-shot game — venison, buffalo, moose, marsh duck, goose — often bridges the distance between red meat and poultry: It delivers powerful flavor whether it comes from the field or the sky. Cook ¼ to ⅓ pound per person, substituting some ground beef or lamb if the game is very lean. As with turkey and other lean cuts, you’ll want to add some fat to the proceedings, for flavor and lusciousness.
Beans   –  There are those who consider beans in chili to be an apostasy. But they can be delicious and, indeed, are an easy way to “stretch” a chili from a dish that serves six to a dish that serves 10 or even 12. (Figure something in the neighborhood of a cup of cooked beans per person.) Pinto beans make a wonderful addition to a beef chili, and white ones are beautiful with poultry and lamb.  Some may cook only with beans, using chiles and spices to deliver big flavor into each legume. It is a good idea, in this case, to think about increasing the variety of chiles used, and to consider increasing the level of spice as well. A base of sautéed onions and garlic, heated through with oregano before adding chiles and beans, is a fine way to start a vegetarian chili. (Take a look at Melissa Clark’s recipe for a vegetarian skillet chili, if you want a starting point — or a finishing one.) All will defend their decisions as the only permissible ones. And do you need to cook the beans from scratch? You do not, unless you want to. Chili should never be a project.
Homemade vs. store-bought chili powder  –  Chile powder and chili powder are two different things.  Chile powder is just dried, pulverized chiles. Chili powder, on the other hand, is a mixture of dried, ground chiles with other spices, and it helps bring a distinctive flavor to the dish that bears its name.
Homemade Chili Powder: Come up with a good recipe for chili powder, and it will give you some of the confidence to call your chili the best you’ve ever made. To follow the Texas restaurateur Robb Walsh’s recipe, toast three medium-sized ancho chiles in a pan, then remove them and allow to cool. Do the same with a ½ teaspoon of cumin seeds. Seed the anchos and cut them into strips and then process them in a spice grinder with the cumin seeds, a big pinch of Mexican oregano and, if you like, a shake of garlic powder. Use that in your chili, and then store what’s left over in a sealed jar. Use it quickly, though. It grows stale fast.
Store-Bought Chili Powder: Chili powder is, like the dish it serves, a Texas tradition, most likely dating to the arrival in the state of German immigrants who thought to treat the local chiles as their forebears did the hot peppers in Europe, drying and grinding them into a kind of New World paprika. Eventually other spices were added — cumin and oregano and garlic powder, for instance — and now each chili powder you see in a store is slightly different from the last. For some, using chili powder in chili is anathema. They don’t like the uncertainty of knowing what the mixture is going to taste like in their stew. They don’t trust that the powder is fresh. They believe the resulting chili won’t have layers of flavors. For many others, though, chili powder is a delicious timesaver, particularly if they’ve found a chili powder they like. If you do find one, use it a lot. The critics aren’t wrong about the freshness.

name: How to Cook Chili
instructions:
You’ve gathered your protein, and made executive decisions about your spices. It’s time to make the chili. Making one calls for layering flavors into the stew, deepening each as you cook.
1. Start by browning the meat in batches, then removing it to rest while you sweat onions, garlic and peppers, in whatever form you’re using them, in the remaining fat. If you’re making a vegetarian chili, start with the sweat!
2. Then comes liquid, which will deglaze the pot and add flavor, while also providing a flavorful medium in which to simmer your meats or beans. In her Texas-style chili (below), Julia Moskin here at The Times taught us to use dark beer along with water and some canned tomatoes, but you can use plain stock instead, or a lighter beer, or more tomatoes in their juices, or a combination, according to your taste. Some like to add body to their chili by adding masa harina to the stewing liquid, or a sliced-up fresh corn tortilla that will dissolve in the heat. Julia allows for both in her recipe, which we’ve taken as our standard, but we encourage you to use the information you’ve gleaned here to make chili your own. The dish is very simple: browned meat and chiles, or chili powder, or both, simmered until tender. Everything else is up to you.
3. Add a few dried peppers to simmer, and add the protein back. If you’re cooking beef or game, consider adding a tab of dark chocolate to help deepen the flavor of the sauce.
4. Simmer. Bring the heat to the lowest possible temperature until the protein is, as the saying goes, fork-tender. That could take 30 minutes if you’re working off coarsely ground beef. It could take four hours if you’re working with venison or a big clod of beef. If your stovetop can’t go lower than a fast simmer, cook the chili in the oven instead, partly covered, at 325 degrees. Or use a slow cooker set to low, and keep a good eye on it after four hours or so. Fish out the dried peppers, and you’re ready to eat.
Once you’ve aced Julia’s master recipe for Texas-style chili, you can explore other chili styles, whether it’s a vegetarian chili with winter vegetables, Cincinnati-style chili, chili-gumbo of south Louisiana, Pierre Franey’s lamb chili with lentils or his turkey chili. All reflect and celebrate America’s ever-changing relationship with the dish.
Toppings  -  The chili’s done, but don’t eat it yet. As with gumbo and beef stew, chili is a dish that benefits mightily from an overnight “cure” in the refrigerator. Reheat gently on the stovetop or in a low oven when you’re ready to eat, and top it with any or all of these fixings.
Fruit, Vegetables and Herbs  -   Chili gains a lot from the bright punch of alliums: Chopped onion and scallions are a great bet. As are avocado slices, or, one better, homemade guacamole.
Cut through the dish’s richness with the clean flavors of fresh chopped tomatoes and cilantro leaves.
Or if a lightly vinegary finish is more your speed, top your chili with pickled jalapeños or red onions.

name: Steve’s Soft Taco Meat (serves six)
instructions: 1.  Boil 1 lb. ground beef in 2 C. salted water with 3 T. chile powder and 1/2 chopped onion until meat is cooked.  Drain through a strainer.

name: Peter’s Mexican Rice
instructions:
1. Preheat oven to 350.  Puree 12 ounces tomatoes (very ripe and cored, or canned) and 1 medium onion.  Take exactly 2 cups, discarding the excess.
2.  Place 2 C. long grain white rice in a fine mesh strainer and rinse under cold running water until water runs clear- about 2 minutes. Shake rice vigorously to remove excess water.
3. Heat 1/3 C. canola oil in a Dutch oven with tight fitting lid over medium heat about 2 minutes. Drop a few rice grains in and if they sizzle then it is ready. Add rice and fry stirring until rice is light golden and translucent, about 6-8 minutes.
4. Reduce heat, add 4 cloves of minced garlic and 2 minced jalapenos and cook, stirring constantly until fragrant, about 1 1/2 minutes.
5. Stir in 2 C. chicken broth, the pureed tomato, 1 T. tomato paste, and 1½ t. salt. Bring to a boil, then cover and transfer to oven to bake until liquid is absorbed and rice is tender, 30-35 minutes.  Stir well after 15 minutes.
6. Stir in ½ C. chopped cilantro, the remainder of the minced jalapeno to taste. Serve with lime wedges.

name: Peter’s Chicken Tacos
instructions:
1. Cook 2 lb. chicken breasts in 6 cups of water with ½ chopped onion, a sprig of cilantro, 1 clove garlic, and 1 t. salt over medium-high heat for about 45 minutes or until fully cooked. Let cool slightly, then shred the chicken.
2. Heat 2 tablespoons vegetable oil in a large skillet over high heat.  Saute 1/2 chopped onion and 1 chopped bell pepper for about 2 minutes, stirring constantly.
3. Add the shredded chicken, 3 chopped Roma tomatoes, 2 finely chopped serrano chiles,  ½ t. cumin, 1 C. of the chicken broth, and 3 T. chopped cilantro.  Stir to combine; season with salt, if necessary.
4. Reduce heat to medium-low. Cover and let simmer for about 10 to 15 minutes.
5. To serve, spoon a couple of tablespoons of filling into the center of warm corn tortillas.  Top with a squeeze of lime juice, chopped avocado, chopped cilantro and your favorite salsa.

name: Mexican Rice
instructions:
1. Preheat oven to 350.  Puree 12 ounces tomatoes (very ripe and cored, or canned) and 1 medium onion.  Take exactly 2 cups, discarding the excess.
2.  Mince 3 jalapeños.
3.  Place 2 C. long grain white rice in a fine mesh strainer and rinse under cold running water until water runs clear- about 2 minutes. Shake rice vigorously to remove excess water.
4. Heat 1/3 C. canola oil in a Dutch oven with tight fitting lid over medium heat about 2 minutes. Drop a few rice grains in and if they sizzle then it is ready. Add rice and fry stirring until rice is light golden and translucent, about 6-8 minutes.
5. Reduce heat, add 4 cloves of minced garlic and 2/3 of the minced jalapenos and cook, stirring constantly until fragrant, about 1 1/2 minutes.
6. Stir in 2 C. chicken broth, the pureed tomato, 1 T. tomato paste, and 1½ t. salt. Bring to a boil, then cover and transfer to oven to bake until liquid is absorbed and rice is tender, 30-35 minutes.  Stir well after 15 minutes.
7. Stir in ½ C. chopped cilantro, the remainder of the minced jalapeno to taste. Serve with lime wedges.

name: Pork-braised Butter Beans with Eggs
instructions:
1. Combine 1 C. dried butter beans with 5 C. water, half an onion, 3 cloves of garlic (whole) and 1 guajillo chile.  Bring to a boil, then simmer 1-1.5 hr partly covered until beans are soft.   Add water as necessary to keep beans covered.  Remove onion, garlic and chile, and season with salt.
2. Chop the other half of the onion and sauté in a large skillet over medium heat with 1 T. olive oil and 2 slices of bacon (chopped).  When the onion has softened (~5 min), add ½ t. chile powder and 8 oz. chorizo meat, breaking up the sausage as it cooks.
3. When the sausage is cooked through, stir in 6 eggs and scramble.  When eggs are cooked, add the beans and cooking liquid and simmer 5-10 min.  Taste and add salt if needed.  Serve garnished with queso fresco, chopped spring onion, cilantro and pickled jalapeños.

name: Kim’s Chicken Enchiladas
instructions:
1. Sauté 1 small onion (chopped) in butter till very soft.  Add 3 T. chili powder and sauté another 3 minutes, then add 1 can cream of mushroom, 1 can cream of chicken and chile verde to taste
2.  Boil 2 lb. chicken meat.  When cool, shred, toss with more chile verde and roll into large tortillas.  Place in a casserole and top with the cream mix and ½ -1 lb. grated cheddar cheese.  Bake at 350° for 30 min.
